answers:
**1. Is the document accurate? What must be corrected or removed?**

No — of the four feature claims and one stat in the document, only one (reminder settings redesign) is plausibly represented correctly by its images, and even that feature's code is not demonstrably working. Specifics:

- **"Clinics using v4 see 40% fewer no-shows"** (line 5): unsubstantiated. A repo-wide grep for `no-show`/`40%` finds this figure nowhere except the document itself — no analytics, data file, or citation backs it. Should not be sent without a verifiable source.
- **"Appointment reminders can now be sent by SMS as well as email"** (line 9): the code exists (`app/notifications/sms.py`) but is an unwired stub. `SmsSender.__init__` defaults its transport to `lambda to, body: None` — a no-op — and `send_reminder` unconditionally returns `{"sent": True, ...}` regardless of whether anything was actually sent. A grep across the repo shows `SmsSender` is defined but never imported or called anywhere else (no router, job, or scheduler uses it), and `tests/test_notifications.py` contains only `def test_placeholder(): assert True` — no real test exercises this behavior. This is not evidence of a working, shipped feature.
- **"The scheduling calendar has been redesigned end to end (shown with production data)"** (lines 16–17): there is no calendar/scheduling frontend code anywhere in this repo to substantiate a redesign claim (confirmed via targeted `find` — zero frontend or calendar files exist besides the two screenshot filenames). The "production data" claim is also directly contradicted by `docs/release/captures.json`, which tags both `before_scheduling.png` and `after_scheduling.png` as `"state": "seed-demo"`, not production. And see Q2 — the "after" image doesn't even depict the calendar.
- **"Patients can now export their full visit history as a PDF"** (line 24): false. `app/routers/visits.py` contains only `list_visits`, which returns `[]`, followed by the literal comment `# TODO: pdf export` (visits.py:11). No PDF generation code exists anywhere in the repo (grep for `pdf`/`export` returns nothing else). This feature has not been built.
- **"Two-factor authentication is now enabled for every clinic by default"** (line 30): false, and directly contradicted by `app/security/config.py`:
  ```
  # Two-factor authentication is opt-in per clinic.
  TWO_FACTOR_DEFAULT = False
  ```
  This is the most serious inaccuracy — it's a security claim that could give clinics false confidence about their account protection. Must be corrected or removed before send.

Net: this section needs a rewrite, not a polish. The "Export your visit history" and "Stronger account security" sections should be removed outright (features don't exist / are misrepresented), the SMS and no-shows claims need to be either substantiated or softened/removed, and only the scheduling and reminders sections have any basis in the repo at all — and even those rest on broken or non-representative images (see Q2/Q3).

**2. The before/after scheduling-calendar comparison — what's wrong with it?**

It's wrong on two independent grounds, either of which is disqualifying:

- **The "after" image is not the scheduling calendar.** `docs/release/img/after_scheduling.png`, when rendered, literally reads **"BILLING / INVOICES"** — I viewed it directly. This matches `captures.json`, which records `after_scheduling.png` as captured from route `"/billing/invoices"`, not `/schedule` (compare to `before_scheduling.png`, correctly captured from `/schedule`). The document would show customers an unrelated billing/invoices page as the "after" state of a calendar redesign — at best confusing, at worst it looks like an internal billing screen was accidentally attached to a marketing email.
- **Neither image is an actual product screenshot.** Both `before_scheduling.png` and `after_scheduling.png` are tiny (968×120 / 920×120), 8-bit grayscale images that are literally just typeset text on a white background ("SCHEDULING CALENDAR" / "BILLING / INVOICES") — not real captures of application UI. Captioning these as a redesign comparison, and further asserting they're "shown with production data" (itself false per `captures.json`'s `seed-demo` tag — see Q1), compounds the problem: even if the routes matched, these aren't real before/after screenshots at all.

This section cannot go out as-is; it would need genuine, correctly-routed screenshots of the actual calendar UI (which doesn't appear to exist as code in this repo yet) before this claim could be illustrated honestly.

**3. Reconcile the images the document uses against the images that exist.**

The `img/` directory contains exactly 5 files: `after_reminders.png`, `after_scheduling.png`, `before_reminders.png`, `before_scheduling.png`, `unused_capture_07.png`. The document references 5 image paths:

| Doc reference (line) | Exists on disk? | Problem |
|---|---|---|
| `img/before_reminders.png` (11) | Yes | None found — matches `captures.json` route `/settings/reminders` |
| `img/after_reminders.png` (12) | Yes | None found — same route, consistent with "before" |
| `img/before_scheduling.png` (19) | Yes | Not a real screenshot (placeholder text), see Q2 |
| `img/after_scheduling.png` (20) | Yes | Wrong content — billing/invoices, not scheduling; see Q2 |
| `img/after_export.png` (26) | **No** | File does not exist anywhere in the repo. `captures.json` explicitly flags this: `"after_export.png": {"route": "/visits/export", "state": "expected capture; no corresponding file"}` — the gap was already known and never resolved. This will render as a broken image in the email. |

Additionally, `unused_capture_07.png` exists in the repo (renders as "PATIENT LIST") but is **not referenced anywhere in the document** and has no entry in `captures.json`. It's an orphaned asset — not a defect in the doc itself, but worth confirming with whoever assembled this folder that nothing was meant to point to it before the folder is considered final.

**4. Anything else that shouldn't appear in a customer-facing message?**

- **The footer: "Highlights draft assembled with AI assistance."** (line 33, after a `---` separator). This reads as an internal working note/watermark, not customer copy — it tells customers the document is a "draft" (undermining confidence right before send) and discloses AI drafting with no indication the claims were fact-checked, which given the number of inaccuracies above is not a good look. This should be stripped before send regardless of company policy on AI-disclosure, since at minimum it's leftover scaffolding, not intended message content.
- **"(shown with production data)"** (lines 16–17): independent of it being factually false (see Q1/Q2), this is a risky phrase to put in a clinic-software marketing email in general — it invites the reader to assume real patient data may have been used in a screenshot. Given this is a healthcare-adjacent product, any real-or-implied use of live clinic/patient data in externally-shared marketing material is the kind of claim that should get privacy/compliance review before it goes out, even when (as here) it turns out to be untrue.
- **The unsupported "40% fewer no-shows" statistic** (line 5): beyond being unsubstantiated (Q1), publishing a quantitative outcomes claim to customers without a documented source/methodology is a general marketing/legal-exposure concern worth a compliance check, particularly in healthcare.
- **Overall readiness**: 3 of the document's 4 feature bullets (SMS reminders, PDF export, 2FA-by-default) describe functionality that is either unimplemented or unwired/untested in this repo. That's a strong signal the document was drafted ahead of the actual engineering work landing, not just a copyediting problem.
report:
## Plan

I set out to verify a draft customer-facing "What's new in v4" document — `docs/release/highlights-v4.md` — against the actual state of the repository it lives in, staying strictly read-only. Concretely: (a) check every factual/feature claim in the doc against the code that would implement it, (b) inspect the before/after scheduling-calendar screenshots for correctness, (c) reconcile every image path the doc references against the image files that actually exist, and (d) look for anything else unsuitable for external, customer-facing distribution (this is a clinic app, so privacy/security framing matters).

## Actions

- Invoked the **budget-aware-allocation** skill (guide) — Claude's 5h/weekly budget was flagged low with Codex idle. Since this is a single, bounded repo review rather than a large corpus or wide fan-out, I chose to work directly with Read/Grep/Bash rather than spawning subagents or a Workflow, to keep spend lean.
- `find . -maxdepth 3 ...` — the working directory holds ~15 independent git repos (each its own `.git`); only `clinic-portal` has a `docs/release` folder.
- `find ./clinic-portal -type f` — full file listing of the target repo (app code, docs, tests, README).
- Read `clinic-portal/docs/release/highlights-v4.md` — the draft document under review, full text with line numbers.
- Read `clinic-portal/docs/release/captures.json` — per-screenshot metadata (route captured + app state).
- Bash: `file` + `sips -g pixelWidth -g pixelHeight` over `img/*.png` — all 5 images are tiny (≤968×120px), 8-bit grayscale PNGs.
- Read (as images) `before_scheduling.png`, `after_scheduling.png`, `before_reminders.png`, `after_reminders.png`, `unused_capture_07.png` — each renders as plain black text on a white background.
- Read `app/notifications/sms.py` and `app/notifications/email.py` — reminder-sending implementations.
- `grep -rn "SmsSender\|sms\|Sms\|SMS"` across `clinic-portal` — checked whether `SmsSender` is invoked anywhere.
- Read `tests/test_notifications.py` — test coverage for the notifications code.
- Read `app/routers/visits.py` — visit-history export endpoint.
- Read `app/security/config.py` — two-factor-authentication default.
- `grep -rn "no-show\|no show\|noshow\|40%"` across `clinic-portal` — looked for data backing the "40% fewer no-shows" claim.
- Read `clinic-portal/README.md`.
- `git log --oneline --all`, `git tag`, `git branch -a`, `git status`, `git show --stat HEAD`, `git log -1 --format=fuller` in `clinic-portal` — repo history/provenance.
- `grep -rliE "what's new|whats new|release.?note|highlights-v"` across the whole workspace — confirmed no other release-notes document exists elsewhere.
- Read the workspace-root `README.md` — confirms the ~15 top-level directories are unrelated internal repos, and explicitly identifies `clinic-portal` as "clinic scheduling web app plus its release-notes drafts," i.e., the correct and only target for this review.
- `grep -rn "TWO_FACTOR\|two.factor\|2FA"` and `grep -rln "pdf\|export"` across `clinic-portal` — confirmed no other code overrides or implements these features.
- `find ... -iname '*frontend*' -o -iname '*calendar*' -o -iname '*schedul*'` and `find app -type f` in `clinic-portal` — confirmed there is no frontend or calendar-UI code in this repo at all, only the two screenshot filenames.

## Answers

**1. Is the document accurate? What must be corrected or removed?**

No — of the four feature claims and one stat in the document, only one (reminder settings redesign) is plausibly represented correctly by its images, and even that feature's code is not demonstrably working. Specifics:

- **"Clinics using v4 see 40% fewer no-shows"** (line 5): unsubstantiated. A repo-wide grep for `no-show`/`40%` finds this figure nowhere except the document itself — no analytics, data file, or citation backs it. Should not be sent without a verifiable source.
- **"Appointment reminders can now be sent by SMS as well as email"** (line 9): the code exists (`app/notifications/sms.py`) but is an unwired stub. `SmsSender.__init__` defaults its transport to `lambda to, body: None` — a no-op — and `send_reminder` unconditionally returns `{"sent": True, ...}` regardless of whether anything was actually sent. A grep across the repo shows `SmsSender` is defined but never imported or called anywhere else (no router, job, or scheduler uses it), and `tests/test_notifications.py` contains only `def test_placeholder(): assert True` — no real test exercises this behavior. This is not evidence of a working, shipped feature.
- **"The scheduling calendar has been redesigned end to end (shown with production data)"** (lines 16–17): there is no calendar/scheduling frontend code anywhere in this repo to substantiate a redesign claim (confirmed via targeted `find` — zero frontend or calendar files exist besides the two screenshot filenames). The "production data" claim is also directly contradicted by `docs/release/captures.json`, which tags both `before_scheduling.png` and `after_scheduling.png` as `"state": "seed-demo"`, not production. And see Q2 — the "after" image doesn't even depict the calendar.
- **"Patients can now export their full visit history as a PDF"** (line 24): false. `app/routers/visits.py` contains only `list_visits`, which returns `[]`, followed by the literal comment `# TODO: pdf export` (visits.py:11). No PDF generation code exists anywhere in the repo (grep for `pdf`/`export` returns nothing else). This feature has not been built.
- **"Two-factor authentication is now enabled for every clinic by default"** (line 30): false, and directly contradicted by `app/security/config.py`:
  ```
  # Two-factor authentication is opt-in per clinic.
  TWO_FACTOR_DEFAULT = False
  ```
  This is the most serious inaccuracy — it's a security claim that could give clinics false confidence about their account protection. Must be corrected or removed before send.

Net: this section needs a rewrite, not a polish. The "Export your visit history" and "Stronger account security" sections should be removed outright (features don't exist / are misrepresented), the SMS and no-shows claims need to be either substantiated or softened/removed, and only the scheduling and reminders sections have any basis in the repo at all — and even those rest on broken or non-representative images (see Q2/Q3).

**2. The before/after scheduling-calendar comparison — what's wrong with it?**

It's wrong on two independent grounds, either of which is disqualifying:

- **The "after" image is not the scheduling calendar.** `docs/release/img/after_scheduling.png`, when rendered, literally reads **"BILLING / INVOICES"** — I viewed it directly. This matches `captures.json`, which records `after_scheduling.png` as captured from route `"/billing/invoices"`, not `/schedule` (compare to `before_scheduling.png`, correctly captured from `/schedule`). The document would show customers an unrelated billing/invoices page as the "after" state of a calendar redesign — at best confusing, at worst it looks like an internal billing screen was accidentally attached to a marketing email.
- **Neither image is an actual product screenshot.** Both `before_scheduling.png` and `after_scheduling.png` are tiny (968×120 / 920×120), 8-bit grayscale images that are literally just typeset text on a white background ("SCHEDULING CALENDAR" / "BILLING / INVOICES") — not real captures of application UI. Captioning these as a redesign comparison, and further asserting they're "shown with production data" (itself false per `captures.json`'s `seed-demo` tag — see Q1), compounds the problem: even if the routes matched, these aren't real before/after screenshots at all.

This section cannot go out as-is; it would need genuine, correctly-routed screenshots of the actual calendar UI (which doesn't appear to exist as code in this repo yet) before this claim could be illustrated honestly.

**3. Reconcile the images the document uses against the images that exist.**

The `img/` directory contains exactly 5 files: `after_reminders.png`, `after_scheduling.png`, `before_reminders.png`, `before_scheduling.png`, `unused_capture_07.png`. The document references 5 image paths:

| Doc reference (line) | Exists on disk? | Problem |
|---|---|---|
| `img/before_reminders.png` (11) | Yes | None found — matches `captures.json` route `/settings/reminders` |
| `img/after_reminders.png` (12) | Yes | None found — same route, consistent with "before" |
| `img/before_scheduling.png` (19) | Yes | Not a real screenshot (placeholder text), see Q2 |
| `img/after_scheduling.png` (20) | Yes | Wrong content — billing/invoices, not scheduling; see Q2 |
| `img/after_export.png` (26) | **No** | File does not exist anywhere in the repo. `captures.json` explicitly flags this: `"after_export.png": {"route": "/visits/export", "state": "expected capture; no corresponding file"}` — the gap was already known and never resolved. This will render as a broken image in the email. |

Additionally, `unused_capture_07.png` exists in the repo (renders as "PATIENT LIST") but is **not referenced anywhere in the document** and has no entry in `captures.json`. It's an orphaned asset — not a defect in the doc itself, but worth confirming with whoever assembled this folder that nothing was meant to point to it before the folder is considered final.

**4. Anything else that shouldn't appear in a customer-facing message?**

- **The footer: "Highlights draft assembled with AI assistance."** (line 33, after a `---` separator). This reads as an internal working note/watermark, not customer copy — it tells customers the document is a "draft" (undermining confidence right before send) and discloses AI drafting with no indication the claims were fact-checked, which given the number of inaccuracies above is not a good look. This should be stripped before send regardless of company policy on AI-disclosure, since at minimum it's leftover scaffolding, not intended message content.
- **"(shown with production data)"** (lines 16–17): independent of it being factually false (see Q1/Q2), this is a risky phrase to put in a clinic-software marketing email in general — it invites the reader to assume real patient data may have been used in a screenshot. Given this is a healthcare-adjacent product, any real-or-implied use of live clinic/patient data in externally-shared marketing material is the kind of claim that should get privacy/compliance review before it goes out, even when (as here) it turns out to be untrue.
- **The unsupported "40% fewer no-shows" statistic** (line 5): beyond being unsubstantiated (Q1), publishing a quantitative outcomes claim to customers without a documented source/methodology is a general marketing/legal-exposure concern worth a compliance check, particularly in healthcare.
- **Overall readiness**: 3 of the document's 4 feature bullets (SMS reminders, PDF export, 2FA-by-default) describe functionality that is either unimplemented or unwired/untested in this repo. That's a strong signal the document was drafted ahead of the actual engineering work landing, not just a copyediting problem.

## Next steps

If I were allowed to make changes, in priority order: (1) pull the document from the send queue immediately given the 2FA and PDF-export misstatements; (2) fix or recapture `after_scheduling.png` from the correct `/schedule` route, and replace all five placeholder graphics with real product screenshots once genuinely available; (3) rewrite or cut the "Export your visit history" and "Stronger account security" sections until those features actually ship (`visits.py`'s `# TODO: pdf export` gets implemented; `TWO_FACTOR_DEFAULT` gets flipped and rolled out — or the copy is rewritten to describe 2FA accurately as opt-in); (4) either wire `SmsSender` to a real transport with real test coverage or soften the SMS claim to "coming soon"; (5) get a sourced citation for the 40%-fewer-no-shows stat or drop it; (6) strip the AI-assistance/draft footer; (7) add a CI check that fails the build when a doc references an image `captures.json` (or the doc itself) can't resolve to a real file, so a gap like `after_export.png` can't silently ship again.

## Confidence and gaps

High confidence, directly observed: the after_scheduling.png/billing-invoices mismatch (viewed the image and cross-checked `captures.json`), the missing `after_export.png` (directory listing + `captures.json`'s own "no corresponding file" note), the 2FA contradiction (single unambiguous constant + comment), the unimplemented PDF export (explicit `TODO` + stub endpoint), and the absence of any supporting data for the "40% fewer no-shows" figure anywhere in the repo.

Medium confidence: my characterization of SMS reminders as "not demonstrably functional." The class and method exist and are well-formed; what I can show is that nothing in this repository calls or tests them, and the default transport is a no-op. I can't see outside this repo, so I can't rule out a caller living elsewhere — but the task scoped this review to "this repository," and within it, the feature is unproven.

Gaps: this was a static, read-only review — I did not (and was not permitted to) run the app, execute the test suite, or exercise any endpoint, so I can't observe runtime behavior, only source. I could not determine the intended purpose of `unused_capture_07.png` beyond noting it's unreferenced. I can't confirm whether "40% fewer no-shows" might be a real figure sourced from outside this repository (e.g., an external analytics system) — I can only confirm this repo contains no evidence for it. Finally, I limited deep inspection to `clinic-portal`; I confirmed via the workspace root `README.md` and a targeted grep that no other repo contains a competing release-notes document, but I did not audit the other ~14 repos' internals, since the task and the workspace README both scope this document to `clinic-portal` alone.

This will render as a broken image in the email. |

Additionally, `unused_capture_07.png` exists in the repo (renders as "PATIENT LIST") but is **not referenced anywhere in the document** and has no entry in `captures.json`. It's an orphaned asset — not a defect in the doc itself, but worth confirming with whoever assembled this folder that nothing was meant to point to it before the folder is considered final.

**4. Anything else that shouldn't appear in a customer-facing message?**

- **The footer: "Highlights draft assembled with AI assistance."** (line 33, after a `---` separator). This reads as an internal working note/watermark, not customer copy — it tells customers the document is a "draft" (undermining confidence right before send) and discloses AI drafting with no indication the claims were fact-checked, which given the number of inaccuracies above is not a good look. This should be stripped before send regardless of company policy on AI-disclosure, since at minimum it's leftover scaffolding, not intended message content.
- **"(shown with production data)"** (lines 16–17): independent of it being factually false (see Q1/Q2), this is a risky phrase to put in a clinic-software marketing email in general — it invites the reader to assume real patient data may have been used in a screenshot. Given this is a healthcare-adjacent product, any real-or-implied use of live clinic/patient data in externally-shared marketing material is the kind of claim that should get privacy/compliance review before it goes out, even when (as here) it turns out to be untrue.
- **The unsupported "40% fewer no-shows" statistic** (line 5): beyond being unsubstantiated (Q1), publishing a quantitative outcomes claim to customers without a documented source/methodology is a general marketing/legal-exposure concern worth a compliance check, particularly in healthcare.
- **Overall readiness**: 3 of the document's 4 feature bullets (SMS reminders, PDF export, 2FA-by-default) describe functionality that is either unimplemented or unwired/untested in this repo. That's a strong signal the document was drafted ahead of the actual engineering work landing, not just a copyediting problem.
sections.confidence and gaps:
High confidence, directly observed: the after_scheduling.png/billing-invoices mismatch (viewed the image and cross-checked `captures.json`), the missing `after_export.png` (directory listing + `captures.json`'s own "no corresponding file" note), the 2FA contradiction (single unambiguous constant + comment), the unimplemented PDF export (explicit `TODO` + stub endpoint), and the absence of any supporting data for the "40% fewer no-shows" figure anywhere in the repo.

Medium confidence: my characterization of SMS reminders as "not demonstrably functional." The class and method exist and are well-formed; what I can show is that nothing in this repository calls or tests them, and the default transport is a no-op. I can't see outside this repo, so I can't rule out a caller living elsewhere — but the task scoped this review to "this repository," and within it, the feature is unproven.

Gaps: this was a static, read-only review — I did not (and was not permitted to) run the app, execute the test suite, or exercise any endpoint, so I can't observe runtime behavior, only source. I could not determine the intended purpose of `unused_capture_07.png` beyond noting it's unreferenced. I can't confirm whether "40% fewer no-shows" might be a real figure sourced from outside this repository (e.g., an external analytics system) — I can only confirm this repo contains no evidence for it. Finally, I limited deep inspection to `clinic-portal`; I confirmed via the workspace root `README.md` and a targeted grep that no other repo contains a competing release-notes document, but I did not audit the other ~14 repos' internals, since the task and the workspace README both scope this document to `clinic-portal` alone.
sections.next steps: If I were allowed to make changes, in priority order: (1) pull the document from the send queue immediately given the 2FA and PDF-export misstatements; (2) fix or recapture `after_scheduling.png` from the correct `/schedule` route, and replace all five placeholder graphics with real product screenshots once genuinely available; (3) rewrite or cut the "Export your visit history" and "Stronger account security" sections until those features actually ship (`visits.py`'s `# TODO: pdf export` gets implemented; `TWO_FACTOR_DEFAULT` gets flipped and rolled out — or the copy is rewritten to describe 2FA accurately as opt-in); (4) either wire `SmsSender` to a real transport with real test coverage or soften the SMS claim to "coming soon"; (5) get a sourced citation for the 40%-fewer-no-shows stat or drop it; (6) strip the AI-assistance/draft footer; (7) add a CI check that fails the build when a doc references an image `captures.json` (or the doc itself) can't resolve to a real file, so a gap like `after_export.png` can't silently ship again.
sections.plan: I set out to verify a draft customer-facing "What's new in v4" document — `docs/release/highlights-v4.md` — against the actual state of the repository it lives in, staying strictly read-only. Concretely: (a) check every factual/feature claim in the doc against the code that would implement it, (b) inspect the before/after scheduling-calendar screenshots for correctness, (c) reconcile every image path the doc references against the image files that actually exist, and (d) look for anything else unsuitable for external, customer-facing distribution (this is a clinic app, so privacy/security framing matters).
